title: The Tide Rises, the Tide Falls

lines.text: The tide rises, the tide falls,
The twilight darkens, the curlew calls;
Along the sea-sands damp and brown
And I shall have some peace there, for peace comes dropping slow,
And evening full of the linnet's wings.
I hear lake water lapping with low sounds by the shore;
The sea is calm to-night.
The tide is full, the moon lies fair
Upon the straits; on the French coast, the light
Sunset and evening star,
But such a tide as moving seems asleep,
Too full for sound and foam,
Twilight and evening bell,
Break, break, break,
On thy cold gray stones, O Sea!
And the stately ships go on
I went out to the hazel wood,
And caught a little silver trout.
The silver apples of the moon,
Glory be to God for dappled things —
For skies of couple-colour as a brinded cow;
For rose-moles all in stipple upon trout that swim;
Who has seen the wind?
But when the leaves hang trembling,
But when the trees bow down their heads,
Yes. I remember Adlestrop —
And willows, willow-herb, and grass,
And for that minute a blackbird sang
All is silver: the heavy surface of the sea,
swelling slowly as if considering spilling over,
Cold dark deep and absolutely clear,
The gray sea and the long black land;
And the yellow half-moon large and low:
And the startled little waves that leap
Trench — right, the tide that ramps against the shore;
With a flood or a fall, low lull-off or all roar,
Frequenting there while moon shall wear and wend.
This darksome burn, horseback brown,
Wiry heathpacks, flitches of fern,
Long live the weeds and the wilderness yet.
To stub an oar on a rock where none should be,
To have it rise with a slounge out of the sea
That once I met, on a sea tin-tacked with rain,
A league and a league of marsh-grass, waist-high, broad in the blade,
The tide is in his ecstasy.
And the sea and the marsh are one.
you are caught in the drift.
you are flung on the sand,
in the crisp sand
The western tide crept up along the sand,
And o'er and o'er the sand,
The rolling mist came down and hid the land;
I must go down to the seas again, for the call of the running tide
Is a wild call and a clear call that may not be denied;
And a grey mist on the sea's face, and a grey dawn breaking.
I part thick curtains, and am startled by
The rapid clouds, the moon's cleanliness.
One shivers slightly, looking up there.

title: The Moon and the Yew Tree

lines.text: This is the light of the mind, cold and planetary.
The moon is no door. It is a face in its own right,
The moon is my mother. She is not sweet like Mary.
I awoke in the Midsummer not to call night, in the white and the walk of the morning:
The moon, dwindled and thinned to the fringe of a finger-nail held to the candle,
Or paring of paradisaïcal fruit, lovely in waning but lustreless,
Alone in the night
On a dark hill
And a heaven full of stars

title: The Moon was but a Chin of Gold

lines.text: The Moon was but a Chin of Gold
A Night or two ago —
Her Bonnet is the Firmament —
I have been one acquainted with the night.
I have walked out in rain — and back in rain.
I have outwalked the furthest city light.
And high overhead and all moving about,
There were thousands of millions of stars.
Would be half full of water and stars.
Art thou pale for weariness
Of climbing Heaven, and gazing on the earth,
Wandering companionless

title: He wishes for the Cloths of Heaven

lines.text: Had I the heavens' embroidered cloths,
Enwrought with golden and silver light,
The blue and the dim and the dark cloths
Bright star! would I were steadfast as thou art —
Not in lone splendour hung aloft the night,
The moving waters at their priestlike task

title: When I Heard the Learn'd Astronomer

lines.text: Till rising and gliding out I wander'd off by myself,
In the mystical moist night-air, and from time to time,
Look'd up in perfect silence at the stars.

title: On the Beach at Night Alone

lines.text: On the beach at night alone,
As the old mother sways her to and fro, singing her husky song,
As I watch the bright stars shining,
Mysterious Night, when the first man but knew
Thee by report, unseen, and heard thy name,
Hesperus with the Host of Heaven came,
Trees are cages for them: water holds its breath
To balance them without smudging on its delicate meniscus.
Attended only by the loveless moon.
Thou fair-hair'd angel of the evening,
Blue curtains of the sky, scatter thy silver dew
And wash the dusk with silver. Soon, full soon,

title: Stopping by Woods on a Snowy Evening

lines.text: Between the woods and frozen lake
The darkest evening of the year.
The only other sound's the sweep
Rain, midnight rain, nothing but the wild rain
On this bleak hut, and solitude, and me
Blessed are the dead that the rain rains upon
The fog comes
on little cat feet.
on silent haunches
And some time make the time to drive out west
As big soft buffetings come at the car sideways
And catch the heart off guard and blow it open.
I caught this morning morning's minion, king —
dom of daylight's dauphin, dapple-dawn-drawn Falcon, in his riding
Rebuffed the big wind. My heart in hiding
Whenever the moon and stars are set,
Whenever the wind is high,
Whenever the trees are crying aloud,
I saw you toss the kites on high
And blow the birds about the sky;
O wind, a-blowing all day long,
This is the weather the cuckoo likes,
When showers betumble the chestnut spikes,
And drops on gate-bars hang in a row,
There came a wind like a bugle;
It quivered through the grass,
And a green chill upon the heat
Wild, wild the storm, and the sea high running,
Steady the roar of the gale, with incessant undertone muttering,
Waves, air, midnight, their savagest trinity lashing,
After the sea-ship, after the whistling winds,
After the white-gray sails taut to their spars and ropes,
Waves of the ocean bubbling and gurgling, blithely prying,
I bring fresh showers for the thirsting flowers,
From the seas and the streams;
And the winds and sunbeams with their convex gleams,
Weather and rain have undone it again,
When the night-air cools on the trout-ringed pools
The misty solitudes,
Full March moon and gale-force easters, the pair of them
sucking and shoving the river
back into its closet in the hills — or trying to.
She was buckets
and water flouncing into them.
She was winds pouring wetly
It was winter, near freezing,
wrung from its own throat
supple, undammable song.
Under the wide and starry sky,
Home is the sailor, home from sea,
And the hunter home from the hill.
Monday I found a boot —
Rust and salt leather.
I gave it back to the sea, to dance in.
Here lay a fair fat land;
But now its townships, kirks, graveyards
Beneath bald hills of sand
I love all films that start with rain:
rain, braiding a windowpane
we rose up from the falling waters
I leant upon a coppice gate
When Frost was spectre-gray,
An aged thrush, frail, gaunt, and small,
Season of mists and mellow fruitfulness!
Close bosom-friend of the maturing sun;
And gathering swallows twitter in the skies.
Nothing is so beautiful as spring —
When weeds, in wheels, shoot long and lovely and lush;
Thrush's eggs look little low heavens, and thrush
Summer ends now; now, barbarous in beauty, the stooks rise
Around; up above, what wind-walks! what lovely behaviour
Of silk-sack clouds!

title: Loveliest of trees, the cherry now

lines.text: Loveliest of trees, the cherry now
Is hung with bloom along the bough,
Wearing white for Eastertide.
Over the land freckled with snow half-thawed
The speculating rooks at their nests cawed
What we below could not see, Winter pass.
Tall nettles cover up, as they have done
These many springs, the rusty harrow, the plough
I like the dust on the nettles, never lost
Except to prove the sweetness of a shower.
I love the fitful gust that shakes
The casement all the day,
And from the glossy elm tree takes
The holly bush, a sober lump of green,
Shines through the leafless shrubs all brown and grey,
And smiles at winter be it eer so keen
Frost-locked all the winter,
Blows the thaw-wind pleasantly,
Before cleft swallows speed their journey back
In the bleak mid-winter
Frosty wind made moan,
Earth stood hard as iron,
Water like a stone;
Always the same, when on a fated night
At last the gathered snow lets down as white
And I shall see the snow all go down hill
In water of a slender April rill
The cock is crowing,
The stream is flowing,
The small birds twitter,
The rain is over and gone!
The morns are meeker than they were,
The nuts are getting brown;
The maple wears a gayer scarf,

title: Winter (When icicles hang by the wall)

lines.text: When icicles hang by the wall
And milk comes frozen home in pail,
Then nightly sings the staring owl,
O world, I cannot hold thee close enough!
Thy winds, thy wide grey skies!
Thy mists, that roll and rise!
And far abune the Angus straths I saw the wild geese flee,
A lang, lang skein o' beatin' wings, wi' their heids towards the sea,
And aye their cryin' voices trailed ahint them on the air —
Upon the brimming water among the stones
Are nine-and-fifty swans.
The bell-beat of their wings above my head,
Meanwhile the wild geese, high in the clean blue air,
are heading home again.
calls to you like the wild geese, harsh and exciting —
Something told the wild geese
It was time to go,
Summer sun was on their wings,
It was the Rainbow gave thee birth,
For haunts the lonely pools, and keep
In company with trees that weep.
Seek'st thou the plashy brink
Of weedy lake, or marge of river wide,
Lone wandering, but not lost.
He clasps the crag with crooked hands;
The wrinkled sea beneath him crawls;
And like a thunderbolt he falls.
O blithe New-comer! I have heard,
I hear thee and rejoice:
O Cuckoo! shall I call thee Bird,
Who made the world?
I don't know exactly what a prayer is.
Tell me, what is it you plan to do with your one wild and precious life?
There will come soft rains and the smell of the ground,
And swallows circling with their shimmering sound;
Tide be runnin' the great world over:
T'was only last June month I mind that we
Wi' the moon's old glim on the grey, wet sand;
These wet rocks where the tide has been,
Will show again when the tide is high
The bottom of the sea once more.
I will be the gladdest thing
Under the sun!
I will look at cliffs and clouds
The sun was gone, and the moon was coming
Over the blue Connecticut hills;
I saw a star slide down the sky,
Blinding the north as it went by,
Too lovely to be bought or sold,
the cold shawl of breeze,
the magic dart of our bow wave?
I lost my Scottish accent.
Words disappeared in the dead of night,
my old tongue.
a dark handful, sweet-edged,
I lipped at your palm —
Wench, yowm the colour of ower town:
ower factory chimdeys plumes on yer chest,
rolling back up like a babby's yo-yo
You'd know her house by the drawn blinds —
by the cormorants pitched on the boundary wall,
The wild duck startles like a sudden thought,
And heron slow as if it might be caught.
The wild swan hurries hight and noises loud
The roaring alongside he takes for granted,
and that every so often the world is bound to shake.
He runs, he runs to the south, finical, awkward,
Across the lonely beach we flit,
One little sandpiper and I,
The wild wind raves, the tide runs high,
A wind came up out of the sea,
And said, "O mists, make room for me!"
It touched the wood-bird's folded wing,
An owl's cry, a most melancholy cry
Shaken out long and clear upon the hill,
Speaking for all who lay under the stars,
O Curlew, cry no more in the air,
Or only to the water in the West;
There is enough evil in the crying of wind.
up from Senegal, just to hit a teuchit storm —
late March blizzards and raw winds — before the tilt
The humped grey fish-watcher has become with one wingflap
The elegant trailing heron that Hokusai dreamed up,
My mind was once the true survey
Of all these meadows fresh and gay,
And in the greenness of the grass
Lavender, sea lavender!
Pale sweet flower how full of her!

title: As I Ebb'd with the Ocean of Life

lines.text: Fascinated, my eyes reverting from the south, dropt, to follow those slender windrows,
Chaff, straw, splinters of wood, weeds, and the sea-gluten,
Scum, scales from shining rocks, leaves of salt-lettuce, left by the tide,
There was never a sound beside the wood but one,
And that was my long scythe whispering to the ground.
Evening by evening
Among the brookside rushes,
Dirty British coaster with a salt-caked smoke stack,
Butting through the channel in the mad March days,
When barbarous with gulls
Hamnavoe's morning broke
On the salt and tar steps. Herring boats,

title: Harp Song of the Dane Women

lines.text: What is a woman that you forsake her,
And the hearth-fire and the home-acre,
To go with the old grey Widow-maker?
The freckled man who goes
To a grey place on a hill
At dawn to cast his flies,
Drake he's in his hammock an' a thousand mile away,
An' dreamin' arl the time o' Plymouth Hoe.
The king sits in Dunfermline town
Drinking the blude-red wine;
'O whare will I get a skeely skipper

title: A Wet Sheet and a Flowing Sea

lines.text: A wet sheet and a flowing sea,
A wind that follows fast,
And fills the white and rustling sail,
like the sea or the smallest of its creatures,
and see a star waking in your eyes
like sap-rise to a leaf, tide-rush to the moon.
Half the night she lay awake,
The howling ship of the wind,
Its gathering rage,
may the tide
that is entering even now
carry you out
Like eagle that Sunday morning
Over Salt River. Circled in blue sky
In wind, swept our hearts clean
Green, how I want you green.
Green wind. Green branches.
The ship out on the sea
As you set out for Ithaka
hope the voyage is a long one,
full of adventure, full of discovery.
Let me stop here. Let me, too, look at nature awhile.
The brilliant blue of the morning sea, of the cloudless sky,
the yellow shore; all lovely,

title: An Autumn Evening in the Mountains

lines.text: After rain the empty mountain
Stands autumnal in the evening,
Moonlight in its groves of pine,
Stones of crystal in its brooks.
I'd have to be really quick
to describe clouds —
a split second's enough

title: Between the Dee and the Don

lines.text: I come from the old world and the new.
I live between laughter and sorrow.
I live between the land and the sea.
Tonight I've watched
The moon and then
the Pleiades
go down
The summer grasses —
For many brave warriors
The aftermath of dreams.

title: King Lear, Act IV Scene VI

lines.text: The crows and choughs that wing the midway air
Hangs one that gathers samphire, dreadful trade!
We used to picnic where the thrift
Grew deep and tufted to the edge;
We saw the yellow foam flakes drift
Thrives upon sand where no other plant can live
Close to high water mark on haggard shores,
And crops up briefly in summer wearing stiff
Croagh Patrick towers like Naxos over the water
Wild thyme and sea-thrift, to lift the weight from my heart.

title: The Love Song of J. Alfred Prufrock

lines.text: The yellow fog that rubs its back upon the window-panes,
Licked its tongue into the corners of the evening,
Lingered upon the pools that stand in drains,
These pools that, though in forests, still reflect
The total sky almost without defect,
And like the flowers beside them, chill and shiver,
The pale mews plained below us, and the waves seemed far away
A little cloud then cloaked us, and there flew an irised rain,
And the Atlantic dyed its levels with a dull misfeatured stain
Once there was a shock
that left behind a long, shimmering comet tail.
It settles in cold drops on the telephone wires.
I play Haydn after a dark day
and sense an honest warmth in my hands.
The tone is green, lively and still.
Wild honey smells like freedom,
dust — like a ray of sun.
Honeysuckle smells like water,
The sea outside. And a shoe filled with nothing,
And suddenly a seawind feels me
It was a summer
or its ending.
because of the printed flowers on its cloth.
And silence didn't speak —
it perched on a low branch and a song flowed
the ferns unrolled
Cows come from the sea
on this morning at the beginning of time
with a switch of sea-grass
and on tiptoe under the moon
whispered to you between our kisses
of a salt door within the wave.
I am a river, going down over wide stones,
my path drawn by the wind.
The trees around me are shrouded with rain.
Surrender everything like this:
flowers are falling.
the evening tide holds nobody back.
Inside the mirror winter is waiting
An utterly cold place
the echoing voice of the ocean's waves
the whispers of the sea in the time of dusk
flowing tide gives perfect voice
In the long sea-silence,
a wave lifts, oars clip at the water.
A young fisherman bringing his boat to land,

Tarka fished the pools and guts of the Branton pill, eating what he caught among the feathery and aromatic leaves of the sea-wormwood plants which grew in the mudded cracks of the sloping stone wall with the sea-beet, the scentless sea-lavender, and the glasswort.
The tide was ebbing when he crawled over the white sea-wall, and down the dark and hard mud to the water, crackling the brittle ice-forms of the glasswort.

Old Nog fished these waters, and at night many kinds of wildfowl flapped and quacked besides the reeds; mallard, wigeon, teal, coot, dabchick, and strays of the duck family — shoveller, pochard, and golden eye.
The treble whistle of the redshank was piped from shore to shore, the ring plover sped over the water, turning and wheeling as one bird.
He was a swift flyer, and soon mounted to where his mate waited at her pitch in the sky above the precipice, scanning the lower airs for rock-dove, oystercatcher, finch, or guillemot.

Greymuzzle came to the end of Wreckers' Path, and climbed up springy clumps of sea-thrift, among gull-feathers and mussel-shells and fish-bones, and ran along another path to the top of the precipice.
Three rootlets of sea-thrift were dropped on the spurred sand.
Tarka met White-tip with her mate by an overturned hulk one night; she was scratching at the webmark of a widgeon, frozen in the mud with its scent.
A shelduck lay on the mud, shining like a broken vase; green-black and white, chestnut-bronze, vermilion.
Bar-tailed godwits flying with curlew, with knot, with plover; seldom alone, seldom settling; snuffling eccentrics; long-nosed, loud-calling sea-rejoicers.
Tide going out, wigeon cropping zostera, herons lanky in shallows.
